title: News Feed System - System Design
description: A step-by-step walkthrough of designing a reverse-chronological social news feed for web and mobile clients, covering post publishing, hybrid fanout, feed retrieval, hydration, media delivery, cache layers, ranking readiness, and multi-region operation.

capacityDiagram: Reads[~100M feed opens/day\n~1.2k avg RPS, ~6k peak]
Fanout[~600M feed entries/day\nif avg fanout is 300]

functional: Users can publish text posts with optional images or videos from web and mobile clients.
Users can view a feed of friends' posts sorted by reverse chronological order.
The system supports up to 5000 friends per user.
Feed retrieval returns enough metadata to render author info, post text, media URLs, reactions, and pagination cursors.
The system filters feed entries based on privacy settings, mutes, blocks, and deleted posts.
The design can notify friends that new content is available without blocking the publish path.
nonFunctional: Support roughly 10 million daily active users.
Keep feed reads fast: target p99 under 200 ms for the first page when the feed cache is warm.
Keep publishing durable: a committed post must not be lost even if fanout is delayed.
Handle celebrity or high-fanout users without overloading fanout workers or feed caches.
Scale media delivery separately from metadata by using object storage and CDN.
Make feed generation eventually consistent; a post may take seconds to appear in all friends' feeds.

capacity.label: Feed opens
capacity.note: Assume 10 feed opens per DAU per day; ~1.2k avg RPS, ~6k peak at 5x.

capacity.label: Posts
capacity.note: Assume 10% of DAU publish 2 posts/day; ~25 avg QPS, ~150 peak.

capacity.label: Fanout writes
capacity.note: 2M posts/day x 300 average eligible friends; celebrity accounts are not pushed to every follower.

capacity.label: Friend limit
capacity.note: Worst-case fanout bound from the prompt; average is much lower.

capacity.label: Feed cache
capacity.note: Store post IDs and timestamps, not full post objects; hydrate on read.

capacity.label: Post metadata
capacity.note: 2M posts/day with small metadata rows; media bytes live in object storage/CDN.

capacity.label: Consistency
capacity.note: Publish is durable immediately; fanout and feed visibility are eventually consistent.

api.description: Publish a post. Media upload can be pre-signed and completed before this call references the media asset IDs.
Retrieve the viewer's feed page. Cursor pagination avoids offset scans and keeps the feed stable while new posts arrive.

messages.label: read post IDs by viewer + cursor

api.description: Create a pre-signed media upload so large images/videos bypass the web tier.
Create a friendship or follow edge. The graph change affects future feed generation.

dataModel.name: users
dataModel.note: Author and viewer profile metadata. Hot profiles are cached for feed hydration.

dataModel.name: posts
dataModel.note: Durable post metadata. Media bytes are external; this row stores references and visibility state.

dataModel.name: social_edges
dataModel.note: Follower/friend graph. Cached in follower/following sets for fanout and read-time merge.

dataModel.name: feed_entries
dataModel.note: Materialized inbox entries for push-fanout users. Store IDs only; hydrate posts and authors at read time.

dataModel.name: media_assets
dataModel.note: Object-storage metadata for images and videos.

dataModel.name: reactions
dataModel.note: Interaction data used for counters, ranking features, and viewer-specific state.

functional.requirement: Users can publish text posts with optional images or videos.
functional.how: The web tier authenticates and rate-limits, the Post Service commits post metadata, media bytes are uploaded to object storage/CDN, and a fanout job is queued after the durable write.

functional.requirement: Users can view friends' posts sorted by reverse chronological order.
functional.how: Feed entries are stored as post IDs ordered by sort_ts. The Feed Service reads IDs from the feed cache and hydrates posts/authors in timestamp order.

functional.requirement: Support up to 5000 friends per user.
functional.how: The Graph Service stores follower/friend relationships and caches friend sets. Hybrid fanout pushes normal authors and pulls high-fanout authors to avoid worst-case fanout spikes.

functional.requirement: Return metadata to render author info, media, reactions, and pagination.
functional.how: The retrieval path uses feed IDs as the skeleton, then batch-loads post, user, media, action, and counter caches before returning cursor-paginated JSON.

functional.requirement: Filter by privacy settings, mutes, blocks, and deleted posts.
functional.how: Fanout filters eligibility at publish time for push entries; retrieval rechecks post status and viewer-specific filters so old or invalid entries do not render.
Notification hints are emitted asynchronously after the post is committed; the publish API returns once durable storage and fanout enqueue succeed.

nonFunctional.how: Stateless web servers scale behind a load balancer; feed cache, post store, graph store, fanout queues, and workers are sharded independently.
Warm feeds are precomputed as ID lists, fetched in one cache call, and hydrated through batch cache reads; media is served by CDN instead of the app tier.
The post is committed before fanout starts. If fanout workers fail, jobs are retried from the queue and feed entries can be rebuilt from posts plus graph state.
Hybrid fanout avoids pushing celebrity posts to millions of followers. Their posts are stored in an author outbox and merged into follower feeds on read.
Clients upload media directly to object storage with pre-signed URLs, and feed responses contain CDN URLs rather than media bytes.
Fanout jobs and notification jobs are asynchronous. Feed reads can fall back to read-time merge for recent posts if push fanout is delayed.

followUps: How would you move from reverse chronological order to ML-ranked feed without rebuilding the whole system?
How would you prevent one celebrity post from flooding the fanout queue?
How would you rebuild a user's feed cache after cache loss?
How would you handle unfollow, mute, block, or privacy changes after feed entries already exist?
How would you keep media uploads from creating posts with broken images or videos?
How would you paginate when new posts arrive while the user is scrolling?
How would you guarantee idempotent fanout worker retries?
How would you measure feed freshness and fanout lag?
What changes are needed for multi-region active-active publishing?

steps.title: 1. Client, Web Tier, and APIs
steps.description: Start with the client-facing surface. Web and mobile clients call a stateless web tier through a load balancer. The web tier authenticates requests, enforces rate limits, and routes publish and read calls to internal services.
steps.decisionPrompt: Define the client-facing contract before internals: what endpoints exist, what synchronous guarantees do they provide, and what work must stay out of the web request?

concepts.definition: A set of web servers that do not keep user session or request progress in local memory between requests.
concepts.whyItMatters: Any request can go to any server, so the tier can scale horizontally and survive individual server failures.

concepts.definition: A policy that rejects or slows requests when a user, IP, or app exceeds an allowed request budget.
concepts.whyItMatters: In a feed system, one abusive publish can amplify into many fanout writes.

whyNow: This fixes the contract that every later component must satisfy.
Authentication and rate limits are cheapest at the edge, before one publish can amplify into many fanout writes.

recap.before: The problem is only a product goal: publish posts and read a reverse-chronological feed.
recap.after: The system has a stateless request boundary, clear publish and read APIs, and internal service ownership.
recap.newRisk: The web tier can accidentally become stateful or do heavy fanout work synchronously.

failureDrills.scenario: An authenticated spammer publishes thousands of posts per minute.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The request path rejects or throttles work before Post Service and fanout queues are touched.
failureDrills.mitigation: Apply per-user, per-IP, and reputation-based limits at the web tier, then monitor rejected publish rates.

strong: Separates public API contracts from internal service calls.
Keeps the web tier stateless and pushes large media bytes out of the request path.
weak: Starts with ranking or machine learning despite a chronological-feed requirement.
Lets web servers do durable writes, fanout, and media processing in one synchronous request.

options.name: REST APIs (default)
pros: Simple, interview-friendly contract for publish and feed retrieval
Works for both web and mobile clients
Easy to cache and observe at HTTP boundaries
cons: Clients may overfetch unless endpoints are designed carefully
Real-time updates need polling, long polling, or an extra channel
Schema evolution requires versioning discipline

options.name: GraphQL gateway
pros: Clients can request exactly the fields they render
Good fit for feed cards with evolving metadata
Can aggregate post, author, media, and counters behind one API
cons: Query cost control and caching are harder
N+1 resolver mistakes can hurt feed latency
More machinery than needed for the basic interview scope

deepDives.title: API boundaries
points: POST /v1/me/feed creates durable post metadata and queues fanout.
GET /v1/me/feed returns a cursor-paginated hydrated feed page.
Media upload gets its own pre-signed flow so large bytes bypass web servers.

deepDives.title: Web tier responsibilities
points: Authenticate auth_token and derive viewer_id.
Rate-limit publishing to reduce spam and fanout abuse.
Keep web servers stateless so any request can land on any server.

bottlenecks.issue: Web servers can become a fanout bottleneck if they do heavy work synchronously.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Commit the post, enqueue fanout, and return quickly.

bottlenecks.issue: Spam publishing can amplify into millions of fanout writes.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Apply per-user and reputation-based rate limits before Post Service.

talkingPoints: Do not start with ML ranking; the prompt explicitly says reverse chronological order.
Keep the first step focused on contracts and request routing, not storage internals.

flows.name: Publish request routing
flows.note: The web tier validates the request, then delegates persistence to Post Service.

flows.name: Feed read request routing
flows.note: The web tier is only a gateway; feed assembly happens behind the Feed Service boundary.

steps.title: 2. Durable Post Store
steps.description: Publishing must be durable before any fanout or notification work begins. Store post metadata in a write-optimized Post DB, cache hot posts by ID, and keep media references separate from media bytes.
steps.decisionPrompt: Choose the publish durability boundary: what record and event are committed before success, and how does the design recover if downstream fanout fails?

concepts.definition: The authoritative storage location used to decide whether a post exists and what its core metadata is.
concepts.whyItMatters: Caches and feed entries are derived from this record; they can be rebuilt if they drift.

concepts.definition: Write the domain record and a durable event in the same transaction, then let workers publish or process that event later.
concepts.whyItMatters: It closes the gap between a successful post commit and a failed fanout enqueue.

whyNow: Fanout can retry, lag, or fail, but the post itself must not disappear after the client sees success.
Separating metadata from media bytes keeps the write path small and reliable.

recap.before: Requests reach internal services, but there is no durable record of published content.
recap.after: Each post has a stable ID, author, timestamp, visibility metadata, and media references in a source-of-truth store.
recap.newRisk: The system now has to handle the gap between a committed post and unfinished downstream fanout.

failureDrills.scenario: Post DB commit succeeds, but enqueueing fanout fails.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The post remains durable and can be discovered by repair or retry logic.
failureDrills.mitigation: Use an outbox table or durable event log written with the post, then let workers retry fanout idempotently.

strong: Defines the post write as the first correctness boundary.
Mentions idempotent post creation and outbox-style fanout handoff.
weak: Stores only precomputed feed entries and has no authoritative post record.
Treats cache writes as durable publish success.

options.name: SQL / relational posts
pros: Simple transactions for post metadata and visibility state
Good fit for moderation, deletes, and author timelines
Indexes make author history queries straightforward
cons: Needs sharding as post volume grows
Hot authors can create write concentration
Large media metadata or counters should not live in the same row

options.name: Wide-column posts
pros: High write throughput and natural partitioning by author_id or post_id
Good for append-heavy timelines
Handles very large post volume with predictable access patterns
cons: Fewer transactional guarantees across entities
Secondary access patterns need denormalized tables
Operational model is harder to explain for a first pass

deepDives.title: Post IDs
points: Use time-sortable IDs so reverse chronological order is easy.
Snowflake-style IDs work well because they embed timestamp and worker identity.
The feed cache can sort by created_at or by the post ID timestamp component.

deepDives.title: Durability boundary
points: The publish API returns success only after the post row is committed.
Fanout jobs can be retried because post data is durable.
The Post Cache is an optimization and can be rebuilt from the Post DB.

bottlenecks.issue: Fanout queue publish succeeds but post commit fails, creating dangling work.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use an outbox table or publish the fanout event after the post transaction commits.

bottlenecks.issue: One storage shard receives too many writes from time-ordered IDs.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Shard by author_id or hashed post_id, not only by timestamp.

talkingPoints: Post storage is the source of truth; feed cache is derived state.
Deletes should mark status and invalidate caches; do not rely on physically removing every feed entry immediately.

flows.name: Commit post then enqueue fanout
flows.note: Fanout starts only after the durable write succeeds.

flows.name: Delete post
flows.note: Feed entries can remain as tombstones; retrieval filters them out while asynchronous cleanup catches up.

steps.title: 3. Social Graph and Eligibility
steps.description: The feed is defined by relationships and viewer-specific filters. Store friend/follow edges in a graph service, cache follower/following sets, and apply mutes, blocks, privacy, and custom audience rules before fanout or rendering.
steps.decisionPrompt: Design recipient eligibility: combine graph edges, privacy, blocks, and mutes into a rule that is safe both at fanout time and read time.

concepts.definition: The relationship model that records who follows, friends, blocks, or can otherwise see whom.
concepts.whyItMatters: The graph is the starting point for deciding which feeds a post can enter.

concepts.definition: A rule layer that removes viewers who should not see content even if a graph edge exists.
concepts.whyItMatters: Privacy, mutes, blocks, and audience settings can change faster than precomputed feed entries.

whyNow: Fanout cannot be correct until the recipient set is correct.
Eligibility is not just graph membership; viewer-specific filters can override the raw edge list.

recap.before: Posts are durable, but the system does not know which viewers should receive them.
recap.after: A graph and eligibility layer can produce the intended recipient set for fanout and read-time filtering.
recap.newRisk: Privacy changes can race with already-materialized feed entries.

failureDrills.scenario: A viewer blocks an author after one of the author's posts was already fanned out.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The blocked content stops appearing even if its ID remains in a feed cache.
failureDrills.mitigation: Apply block and privacy checks again during feed retrieval, and lazily remove invalid feed entries.

strong: Distinguishes graph edges from eligibility rules.
Keeps a read-time safety filter for privacy-sensitive changes.
weak: Assumes fanout-time eligibility is enough forever.
Ignores mutes, blocks, private accounts, or custom audiences.

options.name: Graph service with cache (default)
pros: Keeps relationship logic behind one service boundary
Can cache follower and following sets for fanout and feed reads
Supports mutes, blocks, and privacy filters centrally
cons: Graph cache invalidation is subtle on follow/unfollow/mute
Large follower sets need pagination and sharding
Fanout depends on graph freshness

options.name: Denormalized follower tables
pros: Simple key-value access by follower_id or followee_id
Efficient batch reads for fanout workers
Avoids specialized graph database dependency
cons: Friend recommendation queries are weaker
Relationship changes update multiple denormalized views
Complex privacy rules can spread across services

deepDives.title: Eligibility
points: Publishing fanout must exclude viewers who are blocked, muted, or outside the post's visibility scope.
Read-time hydration should still recheck status because relationships and privacy can change after fanout.
Graph updates invalidate follower/following cache entries.

deepDives.title: Friend vs follow
points: A friend graph is often bidirectional; a follow graph is directional.
The feed model works for both if fanout asks for eligible recipients.
Celebrity handling is usually modeled as one-to-many follow rather than mutual friendship.

bottlenecks.issue: Fetching 5000 friends synchronously on publish adds latency.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Publish only the fanout job synchronously; fanout workers read friend sets asynchronously.

bottlenecks.issue: Large follower sets can create hot graph-cache keys.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Page follower lists, shard by author_id plus bucket, and classify celebrities for pull fanout.

talkingPoints: Graph state is part of correctness, not just recommendation data.
Eligibility must be enforced both at fanout time and read time to handle changes.

flows.name: Follow user
flows.note: Relationship changes update durable graph state and invalidate graph caches.

flows.name: Fetch eligible recipients
flows.note: Fanout workers page through eligible recipients rather than building one giant in-memory list.

steps.title: 4. Fanout on Write
steps.description: For normal authors, precompute feed entries when a post is published. Fanout workers read eligible friends from the graph, then append post IDs to each viewer's feed cache. This makes feed reads very fast.
steps.decisionPrompt: Choose push versus pull for normal authors: justify paying write-time fanout cost, then define queueing, retry, and duplicate-prevention behavior.

concepts.definition: Do the distribution work when content is published by pushing post IDs into recipient feed stores.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes the common read path fast, at the cost of heavier publish-time background work.

concepts.definition: A repeated operation has the same final effect as running it once.
concepts.whyItMatters: Fanout workers retry, so appending the same post twice must not create duplicate feed entries.

whyNow: The feed requirement is read-heavy, so precomputing normal-author posts turns common reads into cheap cache reads.
This is the first point where asynchronous work and idempotency become central.

recap.before: The system can identify recipients but has no materialized feed for fast reads.
recap.after: Normal-author posts are appended into viewer feed caches by background workers.
recap.newRisk: Fanout lag means publish success and feed visibility are no longer the same moment.

failureDrills.scenario: A fanout worker retries the same post after a timeout.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Viewer feeds do not contain duplicate entries.
failureDrills.mitigation: Make feed append operations idempotent with a post_id uniqueness check or sorted-set score overwrite.

strong: States the read/write tradeoff and why push fanout suits normal users.
Mentions queues, retries, idempotency, and fanout lag as first-class concerns.
weak: Does all fanout inline during the publish request.
Does not discuss duplicate fanout or partial fanout recovery.

options.name: Push fanout (default for normal users)
pros: Feed reads are fast because the feed is precomputed
New posts can appear quickly and trigger notifications
Works well when average friend count is moderate
cons: Wastes work for inactive viewers
Hot authors can generate huge write bursts
Feed cache memory grows with active users and retained entries

options.name: Synchronous fanout
pros: Simpler mental model for tiny systems
Publisher knows immediately whether feed writes completed
No queue infrastructure at the start
cons: Publish latency grows with friend count
Publisher request can time out for large audiences
One bad graph or cache dependency breaks posting

deepDives.title: Feed entry shape
points: Store viewer_id, post_id, author_id, and sort_ts.
Do not store full post or user objects in the feed cache.
Limit each viewer's feed list to recent entries such as the latest 500-1000.

deepDives.title: Worker correctness
points: Fanout jobs must be idempotent because workers retry.
Use a deterministic entry key such as viewer_id + post_id to avoid duplicates.
Track fanout lag and dead-letter failed recipient pages.

bottlenecks.issue: A post from a high-fanout author creates a write storm.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Route high-fanout authors to pull fanout in the hybrid step.

bottlenecks.issue: Inactive users consume cache memory.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Keep only active users' feed caches warm and rebuild inactive feeds on demand.

talkingPoints: Fanout-on-write optimizes reads, which are much more frequent than posts.
The publish response should not wait for every recipient feed entry to be written.

flows.name: Async fanout to friends
flows.note: The post is already durable; fanout writes are derived and retryable.

flows.name: Worker retry without duplicates
flows.note: Retries must not insert duplicate feed entries.

steps.title: 5. News Feed Cache
steps.description: The news feed cache is the read-optimized materialized inbox. It stores ordered post IDs for each viewer, not full post objects, so feed reads avoid scanning all friends' timelines.
steps.decisionPrompt: Design the materialized feed representation: what IDs are stored per viewer, how deep is the cache window, and how is it rebuilt after loss?

concepts.definition: A precomputed list of feed entry IDs stored per viewer, usually ordered by time or rank.
concepts.whyItMatters: Reads can page through ready IDs instead of scanning every followed author's timeline.

concepts.definition: The bounded slice of recent feed entries kept hot in memory.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeping only the active window controls memory use and makes rebuilds practical.

whyNow: Fanout needs a concrete destination for per-viewer feed entries.
Storing IDs instead of full cards keeps cache mutation cheap when posts, profiles, or counters change.

recap.before: Workers can compute recipients, but the read path still lacks an ordered inbox.
recap.after: Each viewer has a bounded, cursor-readable list of post IDs.
recap.newRisk: A cache rebuild or eviction can temporarily degrade feed reads.

failureDrills.scenario: A large Redis shard is lost.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The system serves a degraded but correct feed rather than returning corrupt data.
failureDrills.mitigation: Rebuild from recent fanout events, author outboxes, or a durable feed log; cap cache windows to the most recent entries.

strong: Stores compact ordered IDs and hydrates content later.
Discusses retention windows, pagination cursors, and rebuild strategy.
weak: Stores full post, author, media, and counter payloads in every viewer feed.
Assumes cache loss is impossible or harmless without a rebuild path.

options.name: Redis sorted sets (default)
pros: Natural fit for reverse chronological reads by score
Fast trim to keep only the newest N entries
Easy cursor pagination by score and post ID
cons: Memory overhead can be high
Large active-user counts need careful sharding
Cache loss requires rebuild from posts and graph

options.name: Persistent inbox table
pros: Survives cache restarts without a rebuild
Works for inactive users without keeping all data in memory
Can page old feed entries beyond the cache window
cons: Read latency is higher than an in-memory cache
Write amplification is expensive at fanout volume
Needs compaction and TTL policies

deepDives.title: Pagination
points: Use cursor pagination containing sort_ts and post_id.
Avoid offset pagination because new posts shift offsets while a user scrolls.
Fetch a little more than requested to account for deleted or hidden posts filtered at read time.

deepDives.title: Cache size
points: Most users do not scroll thousands of items, so keep a bounded recent window.
For inactive users, the system can rebuild or merge on read instead of keeping a warm inbox forever.
Track cache hit rate and rebuild latency as first-class metrics.

bottlenecks.issue: One viewer with many feed writes becomes a hot cache key.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Shard very large feed lists by viewer_id and time bucket, or move them to read-time merge.

bottlenecks.issue: Feed cache loss causes a cold-start storm.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Throttle rebuilds, prioritize active users, and use persistent post/graph data as source of truth.

talkingPoints: The feed cache is derived state, so the design must explain rebuilds.
Store IDs only; hydration belongs in the retrieval path.

flows.name: Read feed IDs
flows.note: The feed cache returns an ordered skeleton; hydration happens later.

messages.label: drop invalid entries after hydration filters

flows.name: Cache miss rebuild
flows.note: A missing feed cache is rebuilt from recent friends' posts and hybrid pull sources.

steps.title: 6. Hybrid Fanout for Celebrities
steps.description: Pure push fanout is fast for readers but dangerous for high-fanout authors. Use a hybrid model: push normal authors into follower feed caches, but store celebrity posts in author outboxes and merge them at read time.
steps.decisionPrompt: Set the hybrid fanout policy: which authors bypass push fanout, how are their posts merged on read, and what bounds the merge latency?

concepts.definition: Use push fanout for normal authors and pull or merge high-fanout authors at read time.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids queue and cache-write explosions for celebrity posts without making every read expensive.

concepts.definition: Combine precomputed feed IDs with a small set of pulled candidate posts while serving a feed request.
concepts.whyItMatters: The merge must be bounded so celebrity support does not break read latency.

whyNow: The previous push model is intentionally incomplete at celebrity scale.
A hybrid model teaches the core system-design tradeoff: shift exceptional write amplification to bounded read-time merge work.

recap.before: Push fanout makes normal reads fast but can overload queues and caches for very large authors.
recap.after: Normal authors are pushed, while celebrity posts are pulled from author outboxes during feed reads.
recap.newRisk: Read-time merge now has to be bounded, paginated, and latency-aware.

failureDrills.scenario: A celebrity with 50 million followers posts during peak traffic.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Publish succeeds without creating a queue storm or massive cache write burst.
failureDrills.mitigation: Classify high-fanout authors, write to an author outbox, and merge a small recent window at read time.

strong: Names both sides of the hybrid policy and explains the threshold tradeoff.
Limits read-time merge work by recency windows, cursor state, or candidate caps.
weak: Uses one fanout strategy for all authors regardless of follower count.
Moves all work to read time and makes every feed read scan all followed authors.

options.name: Hybrid push/pull (default)
pros: Keeps normal user feeds fast
Avoids write storms for high-fanout authors
Lets the threshold be tuned by queue lag and audience size
cons: Read path is more complex because it merges inbox and outbox sources
Celebrity posts may appear with slightly different freshness
Requires author classification and monitoring

options.name: Pure pull on read
pros: No fanout write amplification
Inactive users cost almost nothing
Relationship and privacy changes are naturally reflected at read time
cons: Feed reads are slow for users with many friends
Requires merging many author timelines per request
Harder to meet low p99 latency without heavy caching

deepDives.title: Classification
points: Use follower count, recent engagement, and observed fanout cost to decide push vs pull.
The threshold is operational, not fixed forever.
A celebrity can be pushed to close friends but pulled by the long tail.

deepDives.title: Merge semantics
points: Read-time merge combines the viewer inbox with recent posts from followed high-fanout authors.
Because ordering is reverse chronological, a k-way merge by timestamp is enough for this prompt.
If the product later needs ranking, this merge point becomes a ranking candidate generator.

bottlenecks.issue: Read-time merge for many celebrities increases feed latency.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Cache merged celebrity candidates per viewer segment or precompute for active followers.

bottlenecks.issue: Classifier mistakes can overload either the write path or read path.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Monitor fanout lag, feed read latency, and dynamically adjust thresholds.

talkingPoints: Hybrid fanout is the central tradeoff in this interview.
Do not push every celebrity post to millions of feeds just to preserve a simple model.

flows.name: Normal author push
flows.note: Most authors use fanout-on-write because their audience is moderate.

flows.name: Celebrity pull
flows.note: Celebrity posts stay in the author outbox and are merged when a follower reads.

messages.label: fetch recent posts by those authors

steps.title: 7. Feed Retrieval and Hydration
steps.description: A feed page is not just IDs. The Feed Service reads feed IDs, merges hybrid pull candidates, filters invalid entries, batch-fetches posts, authors, media, viewer actions, and counters, then returns JSON to the client.
steps.decisionPrompt: Design the read path from feed IDs to cards: batch hydration, privacy rechecks, and fallback behavior when noncritical dependencies are slow.

concepts.definition: Turn compact feed IDs into full renderable cards by fetching post, author, media, counter, and viewer-state data.
concepts.whyItMatters: The selected feed is only a skeleton until these dependent objects are attached.

concepts.definition: Fetch many objects of the same kind in one request instead of one request per feed card.
concepts.whyItMatters: Batching prevents N+1 latency and protects downstream services.

whyNow: The materialized feed is only an index; the product needs complete, viewer-specific cards.
This step exposes the latency budget and N+1 query risk that interviewers usually probe.

recap.before: The system can produce ordered post IDs, including hybrid celebrity candidates.
recap.after: The read path can hydrate a page with posts, authors, media, counters, and viewer action state.
recap.newRisk: Hydration fanout can dominate latency if dependencies are not batched and cached.

failureDrills.scenario: Profile cache misses for every author on a 50-item feed page.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The request batches backend reads and degrades gracefully instead of issuing serial calls.
failureDrills.mitigation: Use batch APIs, request coalescing, small timeouts, and partial fallback fields for non-critical metadata.

strong: Separates feed selection from hydration.
Batches dependency reads and treats counters and viewer actions as separately cached data.
weak: Fetches each post, author, media object, and counter one by one.
Ignores deleted posts or privacy changes during read-time filtering.

options.name: Server-side hydration (default)
pros: Client receives render-ready feed cards
Server can batch cache lookups and enforce privacy filters
Simpler mobile clients and consistent behavior across platforms
cons: Feed Service does more work per request
Large hydrated responses can increase latency
Schema changes affect server and client contracts

options.name: Client-side hydration
pros: Initial feed endpoint can return a compact list of post IDs
Clients can lazy-load details as cards enter the viewport
Useful for experimental clients with different card shapes
cons: Many client round trips can hurt mobile latency and battery
Harder to enforce consistent filtering
More complex client caching logic

deepDives.title: Hydration layers
points: Post cache stores text, media references, author_id, status, and created_at.
User cache stores display name and avatar.
Action and counter caches store viewer-specific liked/replied state and aggregate counts.

deepDives.title: Filtering
points: A feed entry can outlive the post or relationship that created it.
Retrieval drops deleted posts, blocked authors, muted authors, or posts outside current visibility.
Fetch more than the page size so filtering does not return an empty page.

bottlenecks.issue: Hydration causes too many cache round trips.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Batch by cache layer and use request-level timeouts with graceful omission of noncritical counters.

bottlenecks.issue: Post or user cache misses create DB fanout on reads.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use multi-get, cache-aside backfill, and short-circuit if too many misses occur.

talkingPoints: Feed IDs are the skeleton; hydration is where user-visible correctness happens.
Reverse chronological ordering is simple, but the hydration pipeline should be ready to support ranking features later.

flows.name: Warm feed retrieval
flows.note: The common path is cache-heavy and returns render-ready feed cards.

flows.name: Hybrid merge on read
flows.note: Celebrity outbox candidates are merged with the viewer's materialized inbox.

messages.label: fetch recent posts by author IDs

steps.title: 8. Media Pipeline and CDN
steps.description: Images and videos dominate bytes and must not flow through the feed web tier. Clients upload media directly to object storage, workers transcode or validate assets, and feed cards reference CDN URLs.
steps.decisionPrompt: Separate media bytes from feed metadata: choose upload, processing, validation, and CDN delivery states without blocking publish or read latency.

concepts.definition: A short-lived URL that lets a client upload directly to object storage without sending bytes through app servers.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps large media transfer off the feed request path.

concepts.definition: A geographically distributed cache that serves static media from edge locations near users.
concepts.whyItMatters: It reduces latency and origin bandwidth for images and videos.

whyNow: Text metadata and media bytes have very different scale, latency, and failure characteristics.
A separate media pipeline prevents the feed services from becoming file-transfer servers.

recap.before: Posts can reference media IDs, but the media lifecycle is not defined.
recap.after: Uploads go directly to object storage, processors create safe variants, and clients fetch media through a CDN.
recap.newRisk: A post can exist while its media is still processing or has failed validation.

failureDrills.scenario: Video transcoding lags for several minutes after publish.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The feed can show a processing state or omit the media without blocking the whole post.
failureDrills.mitigation: Track media state explicitly, publish only approved variants, and update cached cards when processing completes.

strong: Uses pre-signed upload URLs and keeps bytes off app servers.
Models media as a state machine with validation, variants, and CDN invalidation.
weak: Uploads large files through the same web servers that serve feed reads.
Assumes media is immediately available and safe as soon as metadata is written.

options.name: Direct upload + CDN (default)
pros: Large bytes bypass web servers
CDN handles global delivery and caching
Upload and post publish can fail independently with clear states
cons: Requires media state machine: uploading, processing, ready, failed
Post publish must handle media not ready yet
Security requires signed URLs and content validation

options.name: Proxy upload through app
pros: Simpler client implementation
Server can validate bytes before storage
Works for very small images in early prototypes
cons: Web tier becomes a bandwidth bottleneck
Video uploads can exhaust server resources
Harder to scale independently from feed metadata

deepDives.title: State machine
points: Media starts as uploading, moves to processing, then ready or failed.
Feed cards can hide pending media or show placeholders until ready.
Deleting a post should revoke or garbage-collect unused media objects later.

deepDives.title: CDN behavior
points: Feed responses contain CDN URLs, not media bytes.
Images can be resized into multiple variants; videos need transcoding and thumbnails.
CDN invalidation is needed for deleted or policy-violating media.

bottlenecks.issue: Media processing lag delays feed card completeness.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Publish text immediately, show media placeholders, and update card state when processing completes.

bottlenecks.issue: Abusive media uploads can drive storage and processing cost.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use upload quotas, content scanning, and lifecycle cleanup for abandoned uploads.

talkingPoints: Separate metadata QPS from media bandwidth.
The feed service should treat media as references with readiness state.

flows.name: Direct media upload
flows.note: The client uploads bytes to object storage, then references the media ID when publishing.

flows.name: Publish with media reference
flows.note: The post references media metadata instead of embedding media bytes.

steps.title: 9. Cache Layers and Interaction State
steps.description: The feed path depends on multiple caches: feed IDs, post content, user profiles, social graph, viewer actions, and counters. Make each cache explicit so latency and consistency tradeoffs are visible.
steps.decisionPrompt: Assign ownership and freshness to each cache layer: decide what can be stale, what must be personalized, and how writes invalidate derived views.

concepts.definition: Application code reads from cache first, reads the source store on miss, then backfills the cache.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps ownership explicit: each service decides how to cache and invalidate its own data.

concepts.definition: Replicas or derived views may be briefly stale, but converge after asynchronous updates finish.
concepts.whyItMatters: Counters and profile metadata can tolerate short staleness more easily than privacy decisions.

whyNow: After hydration is introduced, cache ownership and consistency become the main performance controls.
Viewer actions and counters change more frequently than post bodies and profiles, so they need different strategies.

recap.before: The read path knows which dependencies it needs, but not how to keep latency predictable.
recap.after: Feed IDs, post bodies, profiles, graph data, viewer actions, and counters have separate cache policies.
recap.newRisk: Different freshness windows can make the same feed page show mixed consistency.

failureDrills.scenario: Like counters are delayed while the viewer's own like action must appear immediately.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The viewer sees their own action reflected even if aggregate counters are eventually consistent.
failureDrills.mitigation: Read viewer action state from an authoritative or write-through path, and allow counters to update asynchronously.

strong: Assigns different TTLs and invalidation strategies to different data types.
Separates personalized viewer state from aggregate counters.
weak: Uses one cache policy for everything in the feed card.
Requires strong consistency for all counters and metadata without explaining cost.

options.name: Cache-aside layers (default)
pros: Simple and service-owned
Each service can tune TTL and invalidation for its data
Source databases remain the authority
cons: First read after miss is slower
Many layers can create inconsistent snapshots
Invalidation discipline is required on writes

options.name: Materialized feed cards
pros: Very fast reads because cards are preassembled
Lower hydration fanout at request time
Useful for push notifications or email digests
cons: Memory use is much higher
Profile updates and counter changes make cards stale
Harder to personalize viewer-specific action state

deepDives.title: Cache ownership
points: Post Service owns post cache invalidation.
User Service owns profile cache invalidation.
Feed Service owns feed ID cache and can rebuild it from durable sources.

deepDives.title: Counters and actions
points: Counters such as likes and replies can be eventually consistent.
Viewer-specific action state must be correct for the current viewer, so it is fetched separately.
Missing counters should not block feed rendering.

bottlenecks.issue: A single feed request can touch too many caches.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Batch multi-get per cache, limit page size, and degrade noncritical fields.

bottlenecks.issue: Materialized cards go stale when users edit profiles or counters change.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Prefer ID-only feed entries plus hydration for the main product feed.

talkingPoints: Cache layers are not interchangeable; each stores a different kind of data.
State that counters are eventually consistent but deletes/privacy filters must be enforced before rendering.

flows.name: Batch hydrate cache layers
flows.note: The Feed Service fans out to caches in controlled batches, not one lookup per card.

flows.name: Profile update invalidation
flows.note: Because cards hydrate author profiles on read, a profile update only invalidates user cache.

steps.title: 10. Multi-Region and Observability
steps.description: At scale, the feed system needs regional redundancy and operational controls. Reads can be served near users, while writes are replicated. Metrics track publish latency, fanout lag, feed read latency, cache hit rates, queue depth, and media processing lag.
steps.decisionPrompt: Design the operating model: define regional read/write ownership, lag indicators, failover behavior, and the metrics that prove feeds are healthy.

concepts.definition: A measurable target for user-visible reliability or latency, such as feed read p99 under 300 ms.
concepts.whyItMatters: It turns architecture goals into operational thresholds and alerts.

concepts.definition: The delay between a write in one region or store and its visibility in another replica or derived system.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lag determines how stale feeds can become during failover or regional reads.

whyNow: Once the architecture has asynchronous queues, caches, and hybrid reads, correctness depends on observability as much as component choice.
Multi-region design forces explicit choices about write ownership, replication lag, and degraded modes.

recap.before: The single-region design can serve the product path but has limited failure isolation and operational visibility.
recap.after: The system has regional read serving, replicated writes, lag metrics, alerts, and operational levers.
recap.newRisk: Cross-region replication and failover can reorder events or expose stale feeds.

failureDrills.scenario: The primary write region is unavailable for 10 minutes.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reads continue from healthy regions, publishing is either failed closed or routed to a controlled secondary write path.
failureDrills.mitigation: Define RPO/RTO, use durable replication, expose degraded-mode UI states, and alert on fanout lag and replication lag.

strong: Defines product-facing SLOs and maps them to concrete metrics.
Explains regional read locality, write ownership, failover, and queue-lag visibility.
weak: Adds regions without discussing consistency, failover, or data ownership.
Mentions monitoring only as a generic final checkbox.

options.name: Home-region writes (default)
pros: Each user has one write home for posts and graph changes
Simpler conflict handling for post IDs and relationship updates
Other regions can serve reads from replicated posts plus local feed caches
cons: Users far from home region pay higher publish latency
Failover needs careful home-region promotion
Feed freshness in other regions depends on replication lag

options.name: Active-active writes
pros: Lowest regional publish latency
Better availability during regional write outages
Pairs well with globally unique time-sortable IDs
cons: Graph changes, deletes, and privacy updates have conflict cases
Fanout can duplicate or reorder entries if events replay differently
Operationally much harder for an interview baseline

deepDives.title: Regional model
points: Reads can be served close to the viewer because feed caches and post replicas exist regionally.
Writes can go to a user's home region to avoid conflict-heavy graph and privacy updates.
Cross-region replication delay is acceptable because the feed is already eventually consistent.

deepDives.title: Operational metrics
points: Publish p99, feed read p99, fanout queue depth, fanout lag, cache hit rate, media processing lag, and error rates should be dashboards.
Alert on fanout lag before users notice stale feeds.
Track feed freshness: time from post commit to visible in eligible active viewers' feeds.

bottlenecks.issue: Regional outage leaves feed caches cold in the failover region.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Replicate hot feed entries, keep spare cache capacity, and rebuild active users first.

bottlenecks.issue: Replication lag makes feeds stale across regions.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Expose freshness metrics and fall back to read-time merge from replicated author timelines.

talkingPoints: Do not promise strong global consistency for a social feed unless the product truly needs it.
Observability is part of the design because fanout lag and cache misses are user-visible.

flows.name: Home-region publish replicated globally
flows.note: The publish is durable in the home region first; other regions catch up through event streams.

flows.name: Failover feed read
flows.note: A viewer can read from another region while caches warm from replicated data.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end news feed architecture: stateless APIs, durable post store, graph and privacy rules, asynchronous fanout, feed ID cache, hybrid celebrity pull, hydration caches, media pipeline, multi-region serving, and observability.

options.name: Hybrid fanout feed design
pros: Fast reads for normal users through materialized feed IDs
Avoids celebrity write amplification with pull candidates
Keeps post, graph, media, and hydration ownership separate
Observability covers fanout lag and read latency
cons: Read path must merge pushed and pulled candidates
Feed cache is derived state and needs rebuild strategy
Privacy rules must be checked even for precomputed entries

options.name: Pull-on-read feed design
pros: Simpler write path and no large fanout queue
Fresh graph and privacy changes are naturally reflected
Good for smaller networks or low read volume
cons: Feed reads become expensive for users who follow many authors
Harder to hit low latency without aggressive caching
Every read can touch many author timelines
